player/rewards/charm-of-the-dragon-tende.title: Charm of the Dragon-Tender
player/rewards/charm-of-the-dragon-tende.description: Wondrous item, uncommon, requires attunementA small bone-and-vine talisman warm to the touch.Effect Once per long rest, when the warlock deals damage with a spell, they may: gain **temporary hit points equal to 1d6 While attuned, +1 to animal handling of dragonkind and have advantage on initial reaction checks toward the wearer. The charm subtly tightens or loosens with the wearer’s breathing. It smells faintly of ash and clean rain. It grows warm when near dragon eggs or young dragons.

player/rules/alignment.html.title: Alignment
player/rules/alignment.html.description: Alignment is a tool to help describe a character’s values and instincts. It is not a straightjacket, and it does not dictate every decision your character makes. People are inconsistent, and characters are allowed to be as well.Alignment should guide how your character tends to act under pressure, not how they behave in every moment.Characters do not need to share the same alignment to work together, but they should be capable of cooperating with the party.

player/rules/hit-point-increases-in-d&d-.title: Hit Point Increases in D&D 5e
player/rules/hit-point-increases-in-d&d-.description:
At 1st level, characters gain the maximum value of their class Hit Die plus their Constitution modifier.At each level after 1st, a character chooses one of the following:
Roll their class Hit Die and add their Constitution modifier
Take the fixed average value of their class Hit Die and add their Constitution modifier
This choice can be made each time you level up. However once you roll the choice is made for that level, it is final, you cannot drop back to the median after the fact. These averages are rounded up, which slightly favors consistency over risk.

headers.heading: Fixed Average Values by Hit Die

player/rules/house-rules.html.title: House Rules
player/rules/house-rules.html.description:
The campaign will start at level 1 for everyone. Everyone uses Point Buy Player Point Buy Worksheet Everyone starts with 27 points. One ability should intentionally be weaker than the others. Full rebuilds are allowed until the start of Session 2. After this it will have to work narratively with DM approval after this. I'm not opposed to character changes but I'd like to avoid destabilizing the party in the process.
Starting Gold and Equipment – D&D Basic RulesShould a new character need to join us in the middle of the campaign, we will most likely start that character at current player character levels unless the narrative and player/dm agree otherwise.This campaign uses standard D&D 5e rules for falling unconscious and death saving throws.Character death is a real possibility. Choices matter, combat can be dangerous, and caution is sometimes the wiser path. Without meaningful consequences, there is no reason to be careful.Resurrection magic exists in the world but follows normal rules. Time limits apply, required spells must be available, and material costs are in line with published rules. Not every death will be easily reversible, and resurrection is never guaranteed to be immediate or convenient.If a character dies, we will handle it respectfully and collaboratively. Options may include resurrection if circumstances allow, introducing a new character, or weaving the loss into the story in a meaningful way.The goal is not to punish players, but to ensure tension, stakes, and emotional weight remain part of the game.Experience will be done through Milestones, I.E. when the DM says you level up... we level your characters up. This will typically be done at the tail end of a session so anyone who needs help doing so may stay after and get the help they need. We will use the optional flanking rules in combat.When two allies are positioned on opposite sides of a creature and both can attack it, they gain advantage on melee attacks against that creature. This applies equally to player characters and enemies.Positioning and movement matter. Enemies will use flanking when it makes sense, just as players can.
When leveling players will have the choice to either take the default HP increase or roll their hit points at the table (Hit Point Increases in D&D 5e), should you roll a 1 you may re-roll once per level.
Choose your Alignment carefully, I'd like to use these as a guide for how your character will react in situations
Racial Bonuses will be used as written in the 5e Players handbook (Racial Ability Score Increases – D&D Basic Rules)We will not be strictly using encumbrance, but I will likely slow you down some if you start carrying around something which is obviously going to slow you down...We will be using the formula (2 x dice) + mod. Will depending on narrative cause a fumble which may, harm you or someone else you didn't intend, drop a weapon... things like this. I won't be breaking weapons etc. on you. The DM may award Inspiration for exceptional role playing, meaningful character driven choices, or moments that strongly reflect a character’s values.Inspiration can be spent to gain advantage on a single roll and does not stack. For most situations, up to two different characters may attempt the same skill check. Alternatively, one character may take the lead while another provides help, granting advantage on the roll.For group checks, everyone involved will roll. The lowest roll is discarded (when 6+ players are rolling), and the majority result will generally determine the outcome, adjusted as needed for the narrative situation.We use standard 5e resting rules. A short rest is at least 1 hour of downtime during which you can spend Hit Dice to regain hit points. A long rest is at least 8 hours of rest including sleep. After a long rest you regain all hit points and some Hit Dice back. You can benefit from only one long rest per 24 hours.
For full details see the official Basic Rules: https://www.dndbeyond.com/sources/dnd/basic-rules-2014/adventuring#Resting

player/pcs/nixlet.html.title: Nixlet
player/pcs/nixlet.html.description:
Sea Sprite Pet: Nixlet, the TideglimmerCreature Type: Tiny Fey (Aquatic)Alignment: Usually Chaotic Good (but deeply loyal once bonded)Movement: 20 ft. walk, 40 ft. swim, 30 ft. fly (only above water or in misty sea-spray)AppearanceNixlet is a palm-sized sea sprite with smooth sea-glass skin that shifts between teal, pearl, and moonlit blue. Their hair floats like kelp even on land, and their wings look like thin fins made of shimmering water membrane—almost invisible unless they catch the light. Their eyes are huge and curious, like a tidepool at midnight. They wear a tiny sash made of braided seaweed and carry a needle-thin coral spear no longer than a sewing needle.PersonalityNixlet is:
Nosy and fearless (will investigate everything)
Protective of their cleric in a “small creature, big attitude” way
Easily offended by disrespect toward the ocean (or dolphins)
A little dramatic—gasps loudly, clutches pearls, makes silent judgment faces
They don’t always understand “indoor voices,” and they’ll absolutely steal shiny items “for safekeeping.”
Bond With the Sea Elf ClericA sea sprite doesn’t become a pet the way a dog does—it becomes a chosen companion.  Nixlet bonded with Tally after witnessing one of these moments.  She healed a dying dolphin after a storm churned poison into the bay.  After that, Nixlet followed her for three nights, watching, judging, then finally declaring, “You’re… acceptable. I will allow you to be mine.”Tideglow
Nixlet can glow softly like moonlight on waves, lighting up a small area when needed.
Salt-SenseThey can “taste” the air/water and tell if danger is near:
blood in the water
poison
undead taint
storm pressure building
 Bubble Whisper
The Nixlet can create tiny bubbles that carry faint sound—useful for short-range secret messages underwater.
What They Want (and will absolutely demand)Nixlet expects:
a small dish of fresh saltwater each night
to sleep in your hood, hair, or pocket like a cat
to be included in rituals, because they are “basically clergy too”
Pet “Habits” That Make Them Feel Real
Leaves damp footprints everywhere and acts like that’s your problem
Hates dry air and will perch in a waterskin like it’s a hot tub
Laughs silently at serious people
Will absolutely gossip with fish
Nicknames for their “Big Person” 
Tally - when other people are around
Tide Keeper - when they are alone
Sea-sister - when she is feeling affectionate 
Frown-fish - when trying to cheer her up 
Dry-brain - when she is miffed with her
Nixlet, the TideglimmerArmor Class 15 (natural grace)Hit Points 13 (3d4 + 6)Speed 20 ft., swim 40 ft., fly 30 ft. (only above water or in misty sea-spray)STR 4 (-3)            INT 10 (+0) DEX 18 (+4)         WIS 14 (+2) CON 14 (+2)       CHA 13 (+1) Saving Throws Dex +6, Wis +4Skills Perception +4, Stealth +6, Insight +4, Acrobatics +6Damage Resistances cold; bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing from nonmagical attacks (optional—use if you want them tougher)Senses darkvision 60 ft., passive Perception 14Languages understands Common and Sylvan; can speak in squeaks, clicks, and watery whispers (can speak normally while in water)Proficiency Bonus +2TraitNeedle-Coral Spear
Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.
Hit: 5 (1d4 + 3) piercing damage
Tidepin Dart (Recharge 5–6)
Ranged Spell Attack: +4 to hit, range 30/60 ft., one creature.
Hit: 7 (2d4 + 2) cold damage, and the target’s speed is reduced by 10 feet until the start of Nixlet’s next turn.
Bonus ActionsBubble Whisper: Nixlet creates a cluster of tiny bubbles that carries a faint sound message to a creature within 60 feet underwater that can see the bubbles. The message can be up to six words long.Slip Into the Spray:  If Nixlet is within 5 feet of water, fog, heavy mist, or sea-spray, they can move up to 10 feet without provoking opportunity attacks.ReactionsSmall Creature, Big Attitude (1/Short Rest)
When a creature Nixlet can see within 30 feet targets Tally (or Nixlet’s bonded companion) with an attack, Nixlet can flare with moonlit tideglow and spit a furious string of bubbles.
The attacker must succeed on a DC 12 Wisdom saving throw or have disadvantage on the triggering attack roll.
Bonded Companion: Chosen, Not OwnedNixlet can bond to one willing creature (usually Tally). While bonded:
Nixlet always knows the bonded creature’s direction if within 1 mile, as long as both are on the same plane.
If the bonded creature is underwater, Nixlet can communicate with them telepathically in simple emotions + images (not full speech).
If the bond is willingly broken, Nixlet leaves dramatically.
Running Nixlet at the Table (simple rules)
Nixlet acts on Tally’s initiative (same turn), but has their own actions/bonus/reaction.
Nixlet won’t usually deal killing blows unless the party wants that tone.
Nixlet prefers scouting, disruption, warnings, and protecting Tally
Optional: “Pet Chaos” Quirk (Fun Mechanic)Once per long rest, if a shiny object is seen and unattended, the DM may call for a DC 12 Wisdom (Animal Handling or Persuasion) check from Tally. On a fail, Nixlet acquires it “for safekeeping.”

player/sessions/recaps/session-3-player-.title: Session 3 Player Recap
player/sessions/recaps/session-3-player-.description: I knew something was wrong the moment he looked at my necklace. Most people see shells and think of the ocean. Calm tides. Gentle waves. Stories. Mayor Dorrin Flintkettle saw something else. He watched me from across the room like he was measuring something… not my strength, not my skill—something deeper. When he finally approached, his words were blunt, but his eyes were careful. “You look like you know your way around water.” I didn’t answer him right away. Because the truth is… I do and I don’t trust it either. “What do you mean by that?” I asked. He didn’t hesitate. “Water doesn’t forgive.”That sat with me longer than I liked.He told us about the east tunnels—old, abandoned, reopened too soon. Miners had gone in and come back… unsettled. There was water where there shouldn’t be water. Echoes lingered too long, like the stone had forgotten how to hold sound. Tools vibrated in their hands and a sickness no one could name.  I didn’t like any of it. Not the water, not the echoes, not the feeling that something unseen was already moving. The mayor felt like the miners are just goldbricking. Still… we agreed for a price: 120 silver a day for the group. We met the following morning with Foreman Joseph.  He gave us the map and sent us in. The tunnels were… good - solid and reassuring. Dwarven work always is. For a while, I almost convinced myself this was nothing. Then came the dripping. Slow and steady and too rhythmic to ignore. I found myself listening to it more than I should have… trying to place it. Ocean? No. Something else…something trapped. At the sixty feet mark, the stone changed.  Cracks split the walls—sharp, fresh, unnatural, but dry. That’s what bothered me most. If water was coming in… it would be there. Seeping. Spreading. But this? This was like the stone had been forced open from the inside and whatever had done it, hadn’t brought water with it. The rumble didn’t come from ahead of us. It came from everywhere. I felt it before I heard it. Through something deeper and then the walls broke.Three of them came through the stone like it wasn’t there. They weren’t animals. They were something else. Something that didn’t belong in the natural order of things. Not knowing what they are called, we decided to call them Murder Ticks. They trapped us in the tunnel immediately—no room to move, no space to breathe.And then the larger one came: The Monster Momma Murder Tick!  Yikes! The fight was chaos: too close, too loud, too much scraping and cracking and impact. One of the murder ticks refused to fall. We hit it again and again and again and it barely gave out. It became something else then, not a battle but a gradual wearing down. The largest of the ticks—the one we’d labeled 'Monster Momma'—ignored us entirely for a moment, focusing its horrific mandibles on Severance. It hammered against his shield and armor with such vicious, relentless force that the metallic ringing was deafening, a desperate drumbeat drowning out our cries. He was a piece of sporting equipment being used for target practice, standing firm while the creature tried to turn him into a dented can. Finally it stopped moving. We decided to head back to the surface and report to the Foreman and the Mayor.I reached for my magic without thinking. Healing has always felt like balance like the tide but this time, it burned. Not painfully—but warm in a way that didn’t belong. And there was light… purple. Not anything I’ve ever called upon. It flickered through every spell I cast and then I saw it… the creatures turned toward it and suddenly I didn’t feel like a healer. I felt like a beacon.The fight was chaos: too close, too loud, too much scraping and cracking and impact. One of the murder ticks refused to fall. We hit it again and again and again and it barely gave out. It became something else then, not a battle but a gradual wearing down. Finally it stopped moving. We decided to head back to the surface and let the Foreman and the Mayor.Foreman Joseph believed us.  He rang the bell, and the mine changed in an instant. Dwarves moved quickly. Efficiently. Without panic—but without hesitation either.  They knew danger when they heard it. He hadn’t expected to see us again so soon we could tell. But when we told him…when we showed him… he understood. Faedove did most of the talking—smooth, quick, convincing. This wasn’t water creeping into his mine, this was something else entirely.The Next StepThat night, we headed back to the Lantern’s Rest where our room and board was compted for the night.  Later, the Mayor brought us three dwarves; Torvi, Cargun and Dorne. They knew the tunnels but they were afraid.  Faedove did some fast talking and convinced them to join us after explaining she had a spell that would offer them protection.The mayor left, the dwarves finally shuffled out to get what little sleep they could, and the tavern quieted. I watched Faedove settle into her bedroll, still fiddling with the arcane components for the spell she’d promised. The purple light was gone now, thankfully, but the warmth—that wrong heat—still lingered in my bones. I slipped off my shell necklace and held it tight. It felt cold against my palm, a comforting reminder of the ocean I knew, the balance I trusted. Tomorrow, we will go back in. Back into the earth that had tried to swallow us whole. Back into the dark where the stone screamed and the magic was purple. I didn't know what the mayor was measuring when he looked at me.  Tomorrow would be worse, but tonight, I was safe. And with that thought, I finally slept.

player/sessions/recaps/session-1-dm-reca.title: Session 1 DM Recap
player/sessions/recaps/session-1-dm-reca.description: Your investigation into the missing livestock around Edgewood led you far from simple theft or predators.In the woods, you met Sable, a reclusive halfling druid living at the edge of town. She was caring for something unexpected: a small clutch of albino dragons, runts and hatchlings that could not survive elsewhere. What first looked strange quickly revealed itself as fragile and deeply personal.Your search uncovered a darker truth. A hidden group had been using corrupted berryvines to lure and drive malformed dragons south, treating them as mistakes that needed to be erased. When you confronted their leader, Yarnim, the fight took a sudden turn. Rather than fleeing or surrendering, he tried to destroy one of the dragon eggs outright.You stopped him, but not before the egg was badly damaged.Getting the baby dragon back to safety was tense and uncertain. Every movement felt like it mattered. In the end, the hatchling survived.You returned the egg to Sable, informed the town leadership of what was really happening beyond the fields, and returned to Edgewood. The town celebrated quietly. Not everything needed to be said out loud.

player/sessions/recaps/session-1-player-.title: Session 1 Player Recap
player/sessions/recaps/session-1-player-.description: Player Recap Notes from Talieria of the Morning Atol (Tallie)A Personal Account of Edgewood (And Our Teammate’s Brief Career as Sporting Equipment) I would like to begin by stating, for the record, that we agreed to investigate missing livestock. That is a reasonable job. That is a respectable job.  That is not, in any way, a job that should end with our teammate being thrown through the air repeatedly by creatures that, until that moment, I had assumed were mythical, majestic, and at the very least—polite. Edgewood was unsettling from the start.  No blood. No tracks. No signs of struggle. Just animals vanishing like they had collectively decided to leave and not inform anyone.  The forest was worse. Too quiet. No birds. No insects. Just the kind of silence that presses in on your ears and makes you feel like you’re interrupting something. Eventually, we followed the river until we reached the bend in the river.  It looked like a perfectly normal river.  Calm. Quiet. Completely harmless. And then it exploded.  Two pale creatures burst from the water in a shower of droplets and poor decision-making.  Albino dragons.  Small but fast…very, very fast. And, as it turns out, extremely enthusiastic specifically for our halfling. We had just enough time to realize they were looking directly at her before one of them launched forward and— Well. There’s no elegant way to say this. It grabbed her And threw her. Not dropped, Not nudged, Thrown. One moment she was standing on the riverbank.  The next, she was airborne, reconsidering every choice she had ever made, including but not limited to: Becoming an adventurer Walking into that forest Being the right size and shape to be mistaken for recreational equipment The second dragon caught her. Which, briefly, gave me hope. That hope was misplaced. Because then it threw her back. What followed was, by any reasonable standard, a highly coordinated and deeply unnecessary game of catch. Featuring: Our Halfling. They were good at it.  That’s the worst part.  They adjusted their throws, compensated for distance and at one point I am fairly certain one of them added spin. I would like to emphasize that our Halfling did not consent to any of this.  The rest of us, who I had previously trusted with my life, made several important decisions during this time:  they assessed whether I was in danger, they determined that I was not actively dying, and they chose to watch.  One of them laughed. Eventually, she was set down. Gently.  Which, I suppose, we should be grateful for.  The dragons then looked at me. With expectation and excitement.  With the unmistakable energy of creatures who had just discovered their favorite game. This is when Sable appeared.  She stepped out of the forest like she had been observing the entire situation—which, frankly, would explain a lot—and introduced herself as the caretaker of the dragons.  She was calm.  Suspiciously calm, given the circumstances.  She explained that the dragons liked our Halfling.  I have since re-evaluated my understanding of that word. Sable told us she was from the north, where she had been caring for creatures like these in isolation. Recently, however, something had changed. The dragons had grown restless. Edgy. Unsettled by something she couldn’t identify so she brought them south. Then, she told us about the egg.  Stolen and close to hatching.  That shifted things.  We agreed to help and see what we could do. We followed Sable to her hut which was also covered in vines.  Those ruddy vines. Now, I would like to clarify something. I did not touch the vines.  I have learned.  I grow.  The fighter, however, did not.  The berries exploded.  Loud. Violent. Entirely predictable.  We eventually cleared the vines from the cottage and then followed them to a cave.  We tried many ways to get in, but no go.  Eventually we discovered a back door.  Then we saw them…A cult.  Of course it was a cult.  It is always a cult and they had the egg. Cracked. Glowing. Alive. And they were very committed to making sure it didn’t stay that way.  Something about purity. Correction. Removing what didn’t belong. The fight was chaotic. Tight space. Exploding plants. Fanatics with poor opinions. At one point I cast a spell, hit a cultist, and triggered a vine explosion all at once. I stand by that decision. We pushed forward. We stopped them. And then on our way back to Sable’s cabin, the damn thing hatched. Small, fragile and alive.  We brought it back to Sable.  She approached it gently. We allowed ourselves to relax.  Just a little, but we will have to be careful the next time we come back this way.  Those dragons may think any of us can be their next chew toy.

info_allowGlobalGraph.description: Allow the user to view the global graph of all nodes.

info_allowExpand.description: Allow the user to pop-out the graph view to take up the whole screen

info_attractionForce.description: How much should linked nodes attract each other? This will make the graph appear more clustered.

info_linkLength.description: How long should the links between nodes be? The shorter the links the more connected nodes will cluster together.

info_repulsionForce.description: How much should nodes repel each other? This will make disconnected parts more spread out.

info_centralForce.description: How much should nodes be attracted to the center? This will make the graph appear more dense and circular.

info_edgePruning.description: Edges with a length above this threshold will not be rendered, however they will still contribute to the simulation. This can help large tangled graphs look more organised. Hovering over a node will still display these links.

info_minNodeRadius.description: How small should the smallest nodes be? The smaller a node is the less it will attract other nodes.

info_maxNodeRadius.description: How large should the largest nodes be? Nodes are sized by how many links they have. The larger a node is the more it will attract other nodes. This can be used to create a good grouping around the most important nodes.

info_allowResizing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be resized

info_allowCollapsing.description: Whether or not to allow the sidebars to be collapsed

info_rightDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the right sidebar

info_leftDefaultWidth.description: The default width of the left sidebar

info_selector.description: CSS selector for an element. The feature will be placed relative to this element.

info_type.description: Will this feature be placed before, after, or inside (at the beggining or end).

info_featurePlacement.description: Where to place this feature on the page. (Relative to the selector)

info_sourcePath.description: The local path to the source .html file which will be included.

info_allowFoldingLists.description: Whether or not to allow lists to be folded

info_allowFoldingHeadings.description: Whether or not to allow headings to be folded

info_siteUrl.description: The url that this site will be hosted at

info_authorName.description: The name of the author of the site

bodyClasses: publish css-settings-manager styled-scrollbars show-inline-title show-ribbon is-focused
